description: All tests must pass before finishing work. Run the project's full test suite and any relevant linting before handoff or commit. Any failures — whether caused by your changes or pre-existing — must be fixed. Fix all warnings and errors in tests, build output, type checks, and runtime logs. Never silence or defer without a documented exception.
consequence: Broken invariants cause subtle bugs, CI failures, or production incidents

description: Never swallow errors silently. Every caught exception must be reported or logged explicitly, with enough context to diagnose it — never discard an error and continue. (The specific reporting mechanism is a stack concern.)
consequence: Data loss, billing errors, or irrecoverable corruption

description: Prefer correctness over speed. If a code change breaks existing tests, investigate why before adjusting anything. Do not weaken a test to preserve a green run.
consequence: Hidden regressions, broken CI signal, false confidence in test suite

description: Never skip, hide, or remove failing tests to force a green run. A breaking test may be exposing a previously hidden bug. Fix the root cause in the code under test or the test's incorrect assumptions. Do not delete an agent-written test to make it pass — debug the failure and rewrite incorrect assertions; only remove a test if it is truly a duplicate or covers behavior that no longer exists.
consequence: Silent failures, hidden bugs, undetected errors in production

description: Never commit directly to `main` or develop on it. All work happens on feature branches; agent worktree branches merge into the feature branch first. An agent (including AI agents) must merge the feature branch (or its reviewed PR) into `main` automatically once the gate passes — an Opus agent reviewed the ticket and signed off, no open questions or issues remain for the user, and CI is green on GitHub — because passing the gate is the authorization and no separate user consent is required. Never merge unreviewed, CI-failing, or open-question work into `main`. `main` is the production branch.
consequence: Production outage, deployment failure, or degraded service

description: Push your own branch and open its pull request as soon as that branch has its first commit — automatically, with no permission needed, exactly like creating the worktree at the start; being assigned the task is the authorization to push and open the PR, just as passing the merge gate is the authorization to merge. Run `git push -u origin <branch>` and `gh pr create --base <the branch this one merges into>`, and link the task's tracker ticket in the PR body; always pass `--base` explicitly, since a bare `gh pr create` targets the repository's default branch and would aim a worktree branch's PR at `main`. This binds whichever agent is responsible for a branch that carries commits — the implementer for its own worktree branch, and the agent holding a feature branch for that feature branch, even when its commits arrived by merging subtask branches. It is never a licence to push over another agent's in-flight branch, and it never loosens a strictly read-only review constraint that forbids `push`: a reviewing agent does not push, and hands the ticket back instead. Never leave a branch local-only, never wait to be told to push, and never report a task as complete-but-unpushed — offering to push if the user says the word is the same defect as not pushing. Push and PR come before the auto-merge gate and are never gated by it: publishing the branch is what makes review and CI possible at all. If the change is not ready for review, open a draft with `gh pr create --draft` rather than holding a local branch. Only two carve-outs: a repo with no remote configured — ask which remote to use, never invent one, and record the unpushed branch as a work-log todo; and a host guardrail denying `gh pr merge`, which constrains that command alone and never the push or the PR.
consequence: Work strands on a local-only branch where no reviewer, CI run, or teammate can see it, so the auto-merge gate can never pass and the task stalls waiting on a user who was never told anything was needed

description: Tear down your worktree and branch when work completes — the closing step of every task, mirroring worktree creation at the start. Once a change has merged (into its feature branch, or into `main` via the auto-merge gate) or been abandoned, confirm it landed by its squash commit in `main` or its merged PR (e.g. `gh pr view <branch> --json state,mergedAt`), not by `git branch --merged`, which false-negatives here because the harness squash-merges every PR so the branch tip is never an ancestor of `main`. Then, from the primary checkout, remove the worktree with `git worktree remove <path>`, force-delete the branch with `git branch -D <branch>` (plain `git branch -d` shares that same ancestor check and would refuse a squash-merged branch), and run `git worktree prune`. Only genuinely in-flight, unmerged branches and worktrees may persist.
consequence: Merged and abandoned branches and stale worktrees accumulate, so agents cannot tell live work from dead and act on the wrong branch

description: Always read and update the harness work log. Read current context at the start of any work — latest resume note, open TODOs, recent done in `agent_harness.work_log` (via the SessionStart hook, `script/session_context.sh read`, or an MCP query). Before finishing or handing off, write back: `log`/`done` completed items, `add-todo` new work, `set-resume` a fresh handoff note. The harness work log — not chat scrollback or ad-hoc docs — is the canonical home for work-to-do / done / resume.
consequence: Broken invariants cause subtle bugs, CI failures, or production incidents

description: The shared `global`, `global-rails`, `global-expo`, `global-godot`, and `global-unreal` rule tiers are owned centrally in `harness-infra/rules/` and seeded only by `harness-infra/script/build_global_rules.rb`. An app's own seeder writes only its `app=<name>` rows and must abort if `HARNESS_APP` starts with `global` — never seed or rewrite any `global*` tier from an app. To change a universal or stack rule, edit the canonical file in `harness-infra/rules/` and re-run the central seeder.
consequence: Silent failures, hidden bugs, undetected errors in production
